coredata.dc:title: Near-infrared carbon quantum dots from PEG-based deep eutectic solvents for high-accuracy quantitative analysis of naphthenic acids in wastewater
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Hongwei
Liu, Yize
Zhang, Ruoyao
Zheng, Yi
Zhao, Tengda
Han, Jing
Chen, Chunmao
Duan, Erhong
coredata.dc:description: Naphthenic acids (NAs) are toxic components in refinery wastewater and in oil sands process water. The presence of NAs in aqueous environments would cause serious environmental problems. Development of a rapid method for screening and determination of trace NAs is urgent. In this work, a near-infrared fluorescence carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was successfully synthesized with p-phenylenediamine (PPD) and polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG 400) by the hydrothermal method. It showed a good fluorescence response to NAs with good detectability. The limit of detection was calculated to be 0.16 mmol‧L−1, with a linear range from 0.5 to 15 mmol‧L−1. The detection mechanism of NAs and CQDs was also proposed with in situ infrared spectrometer. This work provides new insights into the fluorescence detection of CQDs in the petrochemical industry, broadening the application of CQDs in the detection of environmental organic pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Naphthenic acids
Near-infrared
Orange-emitting
Fluorescence detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism of NAs detection by PEG-CQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (52004080), Key project of National Natural Science Foundation of China (U20A20130), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (E2022208046, B2022208020, B2021208040), Key Research and Development Project of Hebei Province (22373704D).The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for material characterizations.

This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China ( 52004080 ), Key project of National Natural Science Foundation of China ( U20A20130 ), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province ( E2022208046 , B2022208020 , B2021208040 ), Key Research and Development Project of Hebei Province ( 22373704D ).The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for material characterizations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of PEG-CQDs (scale: 100 nm & 20 nm); (b) Particle size distribution of PEG-CQDs; (c) UV-Vis spectra (black), fluorescence excitation spectra (blue) and fluorescence emission spectra (red) of PEG-CQDs; (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of PEG-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths.
FT-IR spectrum of (a) PEG400; (b) PPD; (c) PEG-DES and (d) PEG-CQDs; (e) Local magnification of FT-IR(PEG-DES and PEG-CQDs).
(a) Full scale XPS spectrum of PEG-CQDs; (b) C1s XPS spectrum; (c) O1s XPS spectrum; (d) 1H NMR spectra of the PEG-CQDs; (e) 13C NMR spectra of the PEG-CQDs.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of PEG-CQDs after the action of different concentrations of NAs; (b) Fitting results of the detection performance of PEG-CQDs for NAs; (c) Effect of interferents on the fluorescence performance of PEG-CQDs; (d) Selectivity of PEG-CQDs for detection of NAs.

3D fluorescence spectra of (a) NAs real wastewater; (b) PEG-CQDs; and (c) mixed solutions of PEG-CQDs and NAs real wastewater; (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of mixed solutions of PEG-CQDs and NAs actual wastewater.
(a) Linear relationship between F 0/F− 1 and NAs concentration; (b) Fluorescence lifetime of PEG-CQDs (BLACK) and PEG-CQDs-NAs mixed solution (RED).
Real-time monitoring of fluorescence quenching process of model NAs acting on PEG-CQDs. (in situ infrared spectrometer).
The mechanism of detection of NAs by PEG-CQDs.
Comparison of different methods for NAs analysis.

head.ce:title: Near-infrared carbon quantum dots from PEG-based deep eutectic solvents for high-accuracy quantitative analysis of naphthenic acids in wastewater

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing − review & editing

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Naphthenic acids (NAs) are toxic components in refinery wastewater and in oil sands process water. The presence of NAs in aqueous environments would cause serious environmental problems. Development of a rapid method for screening and determination of trace NAs is urgent. In this work, a near-infrared fluorescence carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was successfully synthesized with p-phenylenediamine (PPD) and polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG 400) by the hydrothermal method. It showed a good fluorescence response to NAs with good detectability. The limit of detection was calculated to be 0.16 mmol‧L−1, with a linear range from 0.5 to 15 mmol‧L−1. The detection mechanism of NAs and CQDs was also proposed with in situ infrared spectrometer. This work provides new insights into the fluorescence detection of CQDs in the petrochemical industry, broadening the application of CQDs in the detection of environmental organic pollutants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As energy demand continues to expand and the shortage of conventional crude oil, heavy crude oil, which is abundant and inexpensive, has gradually replaced conventional crude oil as the main raw material for refining processes. However, naphthenic acids (NAs), a hard-to-degrade toxic compound, are present in large quantities in heavy crude oil. Heavy crude oils from China, Eastern Europe, the United States, and Canada contain large amounts of NAs, and the concentration of NAs can be as high as 4 wt% [1]. If the treatment of refinery wastewater and oil sands process water (OSPW) is incomplete or substandard, NAs will be discharged into the natural environment. The presence of NAs would cause serious pollution to the ecological environment. NAs can be harmful to aquatic organisms when the concentration of NAs in wastewater exceeds 2.5–5 mg·L−1 [2,3]. The conventional methods for the quantitative analysis of NAs include Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), Gas chromatography-Mass spectrometry (GC-MS), and Liquid chromatography-Mass spectrometry (LC-MS). Most of these methods are high cost or require time-consuming pre-treatment processes, for example, derivatization of NAs before detection [4–6]. Although much concerted efforts have been made, it is still an urgent challenge to develop a rapid and convenient analytical approach for NAs analysis.

Compared with conventional detection methods, the fluorescence detection method has attracted more attention for its high sensitivity, simplicity and relatively inexpensive instruments [7]. As a recently emerged optical material, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a kind of carbon particles with a size less than 10 nm. Their lattice structure is similar to graphitized carbon, which is mainly composed of carbon with sp2 structure and carbon with sp3 structure. They own the advantages of strong and stable photoluminescence, chemical passivation, biocompatibility and low toxicity [8–10] in the fields of photocatalysis, bioimaging, drug delivery, biosensing [11–15], and detection [16–21]. Based on good photoluminescence properties, CQDs have been widely used as fluorescent probes in the organic pollutant detection especially [22–25]. Yang et al. developed a facile fluorescence detection system based on ethylenediamine functionalized carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) for the rapid, sensitive and selective determination of catechol under neutral conditions with a detection limit of 0.65 μM (0.07 mg·L−1) [7]. Xue et al. prepared CQDs by ozone oxidation using coal as the carbon feedstock, and the obtained CQDs showed high selectivity and sensitivity for phenol in aqueous solution with a low detection limit of 0.076 μM [26]. Tian et al. prepared CQDs by hydrothermal method using citric acid as precursor, and the synthesized CQDs were functionalized with polyamines to obtain a nanosensor. This nanosensor can detect TNT residues in water with a detection limit as low as 0.213 μmol·L−1 [27].

The fluorescence detection method based on CQDs possesses the superior advantages of visualization and convenience, and thus has the promising potential in the application of NAs detection. At present, the most commonly used CQDs in various applications are mainly short-wavelength emission [28–30]. The short wavelength and poor penetration capability of CQDs limit their usage in the field of contaminant detection. Compared with blue or green CQDs, orange-emitting CQDs could emit longer wavelength and have stronger penetration ability, which is more applicable in detecting pollutants in the complex environment, especially opaque-dark refinery wastewater and oil sands process water. Therefore, the synthesis of CQDs with near infrared emission(Near-infrared carbon quantum dots，NIR-CQDs)has been recognized as the key to expand the practical application of CQDs [31,32].

Deep eutectic solvent (DES) is a kind of newly green and structurally tunable-design ionic liquid. It is obtained by connecting the hydrogen-bond donor (HBD, such as acid, alcohol, amine, etc.) and the hydrogen-bond acceptor (HBA, such as quaternary ammonium salt) through hydrogen bonding [33–38]. Taking advantage of the structure-designable nature of DESs, it is possible to provide targeted and sufficient reaction sites in the preparation of CQDs by optimized the type and molar ratio of precursors. Researchers have found that increasing the properties of π-conjugate functions can promote CQDs emission to redshift from ultraviolet to infrared region. Phenylenediamines, typically aromatic organic molecules, have good conjugated structures. It’s benzene-ring structure is likely to be retained on the surface of CQDs after hydrothermal/solvothermal reaction, which would greatly improve the effective π-conjugate length in CQDs. Therefore, phenylenediamines is optimized as the HBA in DESs. Polyethylene glycol (PEG) is a class of synthetic polymers with a repeating subunit of ethylene glycol (HO–CH2–CH2–OH). The structure of PEG could be commonly expressed as H–(O–CH2–CH2) n –OH. As a class of biocompatible polymers, PEG has many excellent properties, such as low cost, low viscosity, and low toxicity. These advantages make PEG a potential HBD to synthesize DES. Meanwhile, PEG is also a good carbon precursor for CQDs synthesis. This is especially true for those with a small molecular weight (i.e., less than 800 Daltons) since they not only have a high percentage of carbon, but also is subjected to easy dehydration, polymerization and carbonization [39]. Corresponding, CQDs derived from PEG based DES (PEG-DES) is intrinsically less toxic and better biocompatibility.

In this work, a near-infrared orange-emitting CQDs obtained from PEG-DES was synthesized and applied to the quantitative analysis of NAs. The PEG-DES precursor was prepared from p-phenylenediamine (PPD) and polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG 400). PEG-CQDs showed a good fluorescence quenching reaction to NAs after simple mixing with NAs avoid the conventional complicated pretreatment of NAs. The limit of detection was calculated to be 0.16 mmol‧L−1, with a linear range from 0.5 to 15 mmol‧L−1. The novel PEG-CQDs was successfully used in the quantitative detection of NAs in wastewater.

Polyethylene glycol 400, p-phenylenediamine, 1-adamantanecarboxylic acid, cyclohexanecarboxylic acid, 3-cyclohexanepropionic acid, benzoic acid and 2-naphthoic acid were obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. Naphthenic acids (3-Cyclohexenecarboxylic) was obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. Absolute ethyl alcohol and absolute methanol were obtained from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China. Acetone was obtained from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China. Phenol was obtained from Tianjin Ou Boke Chemical Reagent Products Sales Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China. The real NAs wastewater was sourced from an oil refinery in Qinhuangdao, Hebei Province, China. All chemical reagents used in this work were analytical grade and were used directly.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded on an F-7000 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with a 150 W lamp as the excitation source. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-6100S UV–vis Spectrophotometer (Metash instrument Co., Ltd., China) with a 1.0 cm quartz cuvette at room temperature. The Fourier transform infrared spectra were recorded on a Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR) (Bruker Tensor 27, Germany) with wavenumbers from 4000 to 400 cm−1. The in situ infrared spectrometer system (Mettler Toledo, React IR 15, Switzerland) was used for tracing and detecting the chemical reactions over the entire course of the experiment in order to analyze the mechanism and process of the reaction. The properties of CQDs were analyzed by TEM and XPS. The fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs was measured by FLS1000 Photoluminescence Spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd).

PEG-DES was prepared using p-phenylenediamine (PPD) and polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG 400) as precursors. Dissolve 0.1 g PPD into 15.2 g PEG 400 (The molar ratio of PPD and PEG 400 was 1:38), then stir the mixture evenly at 110 °C for 40 min until a homogeneous and clear solution formed. The resultant solution was vacuum dried for 2 h at 60 °C, and then the PEG-DES precursor was obtained.

1 g PEG-DES and 30 mL different solvent (absolute ethyl alcohol, ultrapure water, or dimethyl sulfoxide) were added into a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave. The mixed solution was heated for 12 h under 210 ℃ at a heating rate of 5 °C/min. The product was cooled to 25 ℃ and then filtered through 0.22 µm organic filter membrane to obtain the PEG-CQDs.

3-Cyclohexenecarboxylic was chosen as the model NAs to configure a series of standard solutions of NAs with concentration gradients (0.5–15 mmol‧L−1). PEG-CQDs and different concentrations of model NAs solutions were mixed well within 5 s and then tested for fluorescence emission spectra. The fluorescence quenching rate was calculated based on the tested fluorescence intensity (Define “(F 0-F)/F 0” as the fluorescence quenching rate. “F 0” is the fluorescence intensity of PEG-CQDs at 498 nm excitation wavelength, “F” is the fluorescence intensity after adding different concentrations of NAs solution). The linear standard curve was fitted with the fluorescence quenching rate as the vertical coordinate and the concentration of NAs solution as the horizontal coordinate to construct a system for the detection of NAs by PEG-CQDs.

Common pollutants in oil refinery wastewater include a variety of complex organic compounds such as alcohols, aldehydes, ketones, phenols and benzenes. Among these pollutants, methanol, phenol and acetone were selected as representative interferents in this research. The selectivity of PEG-CQDs for the recognition and detection of NAs under complex environmental conditions was investigated. In addition, the PEG-CQDs were also applied to the quantitative determination of NAs in real wastewater.

To select an appropriate organic solvent, three different organic solvents, absolute ethyl alcohol, ultrapure water and dimethyl sulfoxide, were used to prepare CQDs, respectively. As can be seen from the Fig. S1, CQDs using absolute ethyl alcohol as solvent emit orange fluorescence, CQDs using ultrapure water as solvent emit green fluorescence, and CQDs using dimethyl sulfoxide as solvent emit blue fluorescence. So, absolute ethyl alcohol was selected as the organic solvent for the preparation of PEG-CQDs.

The size and morphology of PEG-CQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as shown in Fig. 1(a). The TEM images show that the prepared PEG-CQDs are spherical in shape and monodispersed evenly in distribution. The diagram (Fig. 1(b)) shows that PEG-CQDs have a narrow particle size distribution ranging from 2 to 3.5 nm, with an average particle size of 2.95 nm.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of PEG-CQDs is as shown in Fig. 1(c) (black line), There are two absorption peaks at 242 and 293 nm, which are attributed to π-π * transition of CC bond and the n-π * transition of CO bond, respectively [40–42]. There is a broad absorption peak between 450 and 600 nm, which may be caused by the n-π * transition of the prepared PEG-CQDs functional groups on the surface (C-O, et al.) [43,44]. The fluorescence excitation (blue line) and emission (red line) spectra of PEG-CQDs were showed in Fig. 1(c), demonstrating that the optimal excitation (λ ex) and emission (λ em) peaks are at 506 nm and 584 nm, respectively. The wavelength range of the optimal excitation of PEG-CQDs is consistent with the range of surface functional groups (450–600 nm).

The fluorescence spectra of PEG-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths from 482 to 510 nm were shown in Fig. 1(d). With the increasing excitation wavelength, the maximum emission wavelength of the fluorescence emission spectrum was red-shifted, indicating a clear excitation dependence of PEG-CQDs [45]. It is most likely caused by the surface state of PEG-CQDs affecting the band gap. The intensity of photoluminescence emission peak is the highest and most stable when the excitation wavelength is 498 nm. Besides, the fluorescence emission wavelength of PEG-CQDs is always larger than the excitation light wavelength, so the fluorescence characteristic of PEG-CQDs is in accordance with the Stokes shift law. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime of PEG-CQDs is 8.59 ns (Fig. S2). The fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) of PEG-CQDs is 14.47% at 498 nm excitation wavelength (Fig. S3).

The effects of storage time, ambient temperature and pH on the fluorescence intensity of PEG-CQDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of the PEG-CQDs decayed to slightly with the storage time. It indicated that the prepared PEG-CQDs had good time stability. Fig. S5 demonstrated the variation pattern of PEG-CQDs fluorescence intensity under ambient temperature from − 2–65 °C. It could be seen that the fluorescence intensity of PEG-CQDs did not change significantly except for a slight decrease at 30 ℃. Overall, the fluorescence intensity remains stable, indicating that PEG-CQDs have high intensity fluorescence performance and a wide range of temperature application. The pH of natural OSPW ranges from 8 to 9.5 [46], and the fluorescence intensity of PEG-CQDs at different pH values from 3 to 12 were presented in Fig. S6. The fluorescence intensity of PEG-CQDs was relatively higher under neutral-base pH conditions and slightly weaker under strong acid/base conditions, but the PEG-CQDs had good stability over an overall wide pH range and could be used for the quantitative detection of naphthenic acids in OSPW without additional pH adjustment.

FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to determine the surface functional groups of PEG-CQDs. Fig. 2(a) and (b) were the FT-IR spectra of PEG 400 and PPD, respectively. The stretching vibrations of C-O, C-H and O-H bonds and the bending vibrations of C-H in the methylene group are present in PEG 400 (Fig. 2(a)). The stretching vibration of C-N bond, stretching and bending vibration of C-H bond, stretching vibration of CC bond belonging to benzene ring skeleton vibration, and stretching vibration and bending vibration of N-H bond belonging to -NH2- in PPD all can be determined (Fig. 2(b)). The FT-IR spectral results of these two precursors are fully consistent with the structural features of both. Fig. 2(c) showed the FT-IR spectra of PEG-DES. The peaks at 3381 cm−1 and 1352 cm−1 represent the stretching and bending vibrations of the O-H bond belonging to ROH. The peak at 1455 cm−1 ascribe to vibrations of benzene rings. The peak at 1249 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the C-N bond of PPD. The peak at 1106 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration of the C-O bond. Compared with precursors, the vibration of the chemical bonds of PEG-DES does not change much. Besides, the bending vibrations of the N-H bond attributed to -NH2- and the CC bond stretching vibrations of the benzene ring skeleton vibrations are included in the range around 1641 cm−1 and 1517 cm−1. The peak intensity is weaker relative to the two precursors, which may be caused by the hydrogen bonding between PEG 400 and PPD in the formation of PEG-DES.

Fig. 2(d) showed the FT-IR spectra of PEG-CQDs. Compared with Fig. 2(a), (b) and (c), the characteristic stretching vibration peaks of -NH2, O-H, C-H, C-N, and C-O in Fig. 2(d) are still present, only with reduced intensity. The vibration belonging to the benzene ring at 1462 cm−1 is slightly shifted compared to PEG-DES (1458 cm−1), it shows that PEG-CQDs surface has π-π conjugated structure brought by benzene ring [44,47]. FT-IR results shows that PEG-CQDs have conjugated benzene structures, nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing groups, which promote PEG-CQDs has a high quantum yield and ideal hydrophilicity [48]. It is helpful to react with pollutants in the water environment and provides a theoretical basis for the detection of NAs in wastewater.

To further explore the mechanism of CQDs generation and the surface functional groups, the experiments were also tested by XPS in Fig. 3(a, b, c). There are two distinct peaks in the full XPS spectrum of Fig. 3(a). The peaks located at 285 eV and 532 eV represent the two elements of C and O in the sample, respectively. With data fitting analysis, the chemical bond composition of C and O elements in PEG-CQDs were shown in Fig. 3(b) and (c). The C1s fine spectrum is fitted to obtain three split peaks with binding energies around 283.2 eV, 284.7 eV, and 285.1 eV, which represent sp2 carbon, C-C/C-H, and C-N bonds, respectively [49,50]. O1s spectrum in Fig. 3(c) is successfully fitted as a peak with a binding energy of 531.5 eV, which is attributed to the C-O/CO bond [49]. 1H NMR and 13C NMR were used to characterize the types of hydrogen and carbon in PEG-CQDs as shown in Fig. 3(d-e). It can be seen from the 1H NMR spectrum in Fig. 3(d) that the triplet at 1.08 ppm and the split peak at 3.52–3.60 ppm of N-CQDs may represent the characteristic hydrogen atoms in C-H; the peak at 4.72 ppm may represent the characteristic hydrogen atoms in -NH2. The 13C NMR spectrum in Fig. 3(e) includes one peak and one peak cluster. The response peak located at 16.8 ppm represents the C-N bond. The response peak clusters located in the range of 55–72 ppm represent mainly C-OH, C-O-C, and C atoms in CC. The results of XPS and NMR confirm that the surface of PEG-CQDs is rich in functional groups.

3-Cyclohexenecarboxylic was chosen as the model NAs to configure a series of standard solutions of NAs with concentration gradients (0.5–15 mmol‧L−1). After mixing PEG-CQDs and different concentrations of NAs solutions at a certain ratio, the fluorescence emission spectra of the mixture were characterized to study the fluorescence detection performance of PEG-CQDs on NAs. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at 498 nm excitation light with the concentration range of 0.5–15 mmol‧L−1 ( Fig. 4(a)). It could be seen that PEG-CQDs exhibited a significant quenching after the addition of model NAs solution. The fluorescence intensity of PEG-CQDs showed a clear hierarchical gradual fluorescence quenching with the increasing concentration of NAs solution. It may be due to the combination of NAs with the groups on the surface of PEG-CQDs, which would inhibit the fluorescence of PEG-CQDs. The detection performance of PEG-CQDs for NAs was fitted in Fig. 4(b). The fitted results showed good linearity in the concentration range of NAs from 0.5 mmol‧L−1 to 15 mmol‧L−1. The linear relationship equation was (F 0-F)/F 0 = 0.32x + 61.15, R2 = 0.98981, and the calculated detection limit was 0.16 mmol‧L−1 [7]. In order to verify the reproducibility of the detection, experiments were repeated three times and shown in the form of error bars in Fig. 4(b).

Fig. S7 shows the fluorescence images of PEG-CQDs (left 1) and the mixed solution of PEG-CQDs and model NAs solution with different concentrations under 365 nm UV light. The fluorescence quenching effect of PEG-CQDs was gradually enhanced with the increasing of NAs concentration.

Series of model NAs with different alkyl chain length or aromatic, such as 1-adamantanecarboxylic acid, cyclohexanecarboxylic acid, 3-cyclohexanepropionic acid, benzoic acid, 2-naphthoic acid, were also analyzed as a systematic study (Fig. S8). And the results showed all NAs could be analyzed with good accuracy under mild conditions (Table S1). PEG-CQDs are therefore an efficient probe for NAs, and it could be promising used in the actual wastewater with complex types of naphthenic acids.

In addition, the effects of other interferents on the fluorescence properties of PEG-CQDs were also investigated under the same conditions. Fig. 4(c-d) showed the fluorescence spectra and (F 0-F)/F 0 images of PEG-CQDs mixed with model NAs, phenol, acetone, and methanol, respectively. The quenching rate of fluorescence intensity of PEG-CQDs was significantly stronger than that of other three interferents. It indicated that only NAs had the best quenching effect on PEG-CQDs among all substances, and PEG-CQDs had good selectivity for the recognition detection of NAs.

The PEG-CQDs were also applied to the quantitative determination of NAs in actual wastewater. Fig. 5(a-c) showed the 3D fluorescence spectra of actual wastewater, PEG-CQDs and the mixed solution of PEG-CQDs and actual wastewater, respectively. The fluorescence center of wastewater water sample is concentrated at (λ ex300 nm, λ em360 nm) (Fig. 5(a)), and there is no fluorescence emission at λ ex498 nm. PEG-CQDs have a distinct fluorescence center at (λ ex498 nm, λ em585 nm) (Fig. 5(b)), which does not overlap with the fluorescence center of wastewater. When PEG-CQDs were mixed with actual wastewater, the fluorescence center of PEG-CQDs located at (λ ex 498 nm, λ em 585 nm) disappeared (Fig. 5(c)). It is most likely due to the interaction of the NAs in actual wastewater with the functional groups on the surface of PEG-CQDs, which led to the disappearance of the fluorescence centers of PEG-CQDs and produces an obvious fluorescence quenching. Fig. 5(d) also confirms this conclusion. According to the fluorescence quenching relationship (F 0-F)/F 0 = 0.32x + 61.15, the corresponding NAs concentration was calculated to 0.082 mol‧ L−1. After the experiment was repeated for 10 times, the standard deviation and relative standard deviation were calculated based on the experimental results (Table S2). The relative standard deviation (1.539%) has proved the PEG-CQDs for NAs analysis merits great precision. The accuracy could be confirmed by the recovery test (Table S3). The recovery rate was 96–102% when 1–2 times standard was added to the actual wastewater (about 0.015 mol‧L−1). Considering the above research results, the performance of PEG-CQDs analysis were compared with the conventional methods [51–54] ( Table 1), and it exhibited superiority for the specific NAs detection in actual wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of NAs detection by PEG-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Furthermore, the photoluminescence quenching was analyzed using the Stern−Volmer model:[55,56].
(1)
F 0/F–1 = K SV[Q] = k q τ 0[Q]
where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the PEG-CQDs in the absence and in the presence of NAs, respectively; K SV is the Stern−Volmer quenching constant; [Q] is the concentration of NAs; k q is the quenching rate constant, and τ 0 is the fluorescence lifetime of the PEG-CQDs.

As presented in Fig. 6(a), (F 0/F−1) showed a linear correlation with the concentration of NAs between 0.5 and 15 mM (F 0/F–1 = 0.023 [NAs] + 1.579), suggesting that the Stern−Volmer quenching constant K SV = 0.023 mM−1. Taking τ 0 (8.59 ns) and K SV (0.023 mM−1) into formula (1), k q is calculated to be 2.68 × 109 M−1 s−1, which is lower than the maximum value (1.0 × 1010) of dynamic quenching, verifying dynamic quenching is likely to happen in the detection system of this study [55].

The fluorescence lifetime of PEG-CQDs and PEG-CQDs-NAs mixed solution were tested. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the black line and red line represent the fluorescence lifetime curves of PEG-CQDs (8.59 ns) and PEG-CQDs-NAs (7.58 ns) mixed solution, respectively. After the addition of NAs, the fluorescence lifetime of PEG-CQDs was shortened from 8.59 ns to 7.58 ns, and the fluorescence lifetime was reduced by 12%, which also proved the existence of dynamic quenching mechanism [40,57]. In conclusion, the fluorescence quenching caused by NAs on PEG-CQDs may result from the dynamic quenching caused by NAs colliding with PEG-CQDs during molecular motion [58].

To further verify the above views, the results of UV–vis absorption spectra and 3D fluorescence spectra are integrated, and the fluorescence quenching of PEG-CQDs by model NAs was monitored in real time by in situ infrared spectrometer. In Fig. 1(c), the wavelength range of the optimal excitation of PEG-CQDs is consistent with the range of surface functional groups (450–600 nm), and Fig. 5(b) shown that PEG-CQDs have a distinct fluorescence center at (λ ex 498 nm, λ em 585 nm). It is suggested that the fluorescence emission of PEG-CQDs may originate from its surface functional groups, the fluorescence quenching detection of NAs may be based on this.

As shown in Fig. 7, the PEG-CQDs solution was firstly added, and then a certain amount of model NAs solution was added after 30 min. Thereafter, NAs were added every 30 min, and the overall experiment duration was 4 h. According to the locally enlarged figure (Fig. 7(b) and (c)) in Fig. 7(a), it can be clearly observed that, compared with the original absorption peak of PEG-CQDs, a new absorption peak appears at 1704 cm−1 after the addition of NAs (Fig. 7(b)), and it increases with the addition of NAs. This absorption peak belongs to the CO bond of R-COOH class, corresponding to the structural formula of model NAs. The wavelength bands of 1000–1100 cm−1 and 1150–1250 cm−1 represent the surface functional groups such as C-O bond and C-N bond(Fig. 7(c), (d)). The intensity of the absorption peak decreases with the gradual addition of NAs, it may result from the dynamic quenching due to the collision of NAs with the surface functional groups of PEG-CQDs during molecular movement. On this basis, the mechanism of detection of NAs by PEG-CQDs is postulated in Fig. 8.

In this work, a near-infrared orange-emitting carbon quantum dots (PEG-CQDs) with near-infrared emission based on Polyethylene glycol 400 and p-phenylenediamine was synthesized by hydrothermal method. The fluorescence of PEG-CQDs was successfully used to establish a quantitative detection system for naphthenic acids. The assay has a low detection limit and is extremely environmentally tolerant. In addition, the mechanism of PEG-CQDs for the detection of naphthenic acids was also investigated. The application of PEG-CQDs for the quantitative detection of naphthenic acids studied in this paper provides new insights into the application of CQDs in the petrochemical industry. To a certain extent, it has important basic value to broaden the application range of CQDs in the detection of environmental organic pollutants.

Hongwei Ren: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing − review & editing. Yize Liu: Methodology, Validation, Writing − original draft preparation. Ruoyao Zhang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yi Zheng: Investigation. Tengda Zhao: Investigation. Jing Han: Investigation. Chunmao Chen: Investigation. Erhong Duan: Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (52004080), Key project of National Natural Science Foundation of China (U20A20130), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (E2022208046, B2022208020, B2021208040), Key Research and Development Project of Hebei Province (22373704D).The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for material characterizations.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2023.109988.
